text: Standard laparoscopic ports are placed after the abdomen has been insufflated with a varus needle to 15 millimeters of mercury pressure.
A liver retractor is placed through a right subcostal port and used to elevate the left lateral segment of the liver, further revealing the large parasophageal hernia you see below.
The dissection is begun by mobilizing the peritoneum off of the diaphragm anteriorly and towards the right cruce of the diaphragm.
This will allow for the beginnings of mobilization of the hernia sac.
which will need to be completely excised during the course of the operation.
The hernia sac may be further mobilized anteriorly separating the sac from the loose areolar tissue of the mediastinum.
Much of this dissection may be done with harmonic scalpel as well as with gentle blunt dissection.
The anterior sac may be bisected to further assist with mobilization of the incarcerated organs as well as further mobilization of the hernia sac from the mediastinum.
With much of the anterior sac excised, the viscera and spleen may be reduced from the hernia back to their proper position in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen.
Further dissection and excision of the hernia sac may more easily be performed now along the right cruz of the diaphragm.
The left sided hernia sac along with elongated
Sure gastric vessels may then be dissected and divided at this time.
A Penrose drain may be used to facilitate dissection.
Here the esophagus is clearly foreshortened as the GE junction lies at the level of the hiatus.
A 54 French bougie may be placed by anesthesia into the stomach under direct visualization.
A gastric wedge fundectomy may then be performed by firing an endogia stapler into the bougie.
A second firing of the stapler should be aligned flush with the bougie to create a tubularized neoesophagus.
A final staple firing is used to complete the wedge fundectomy.
Idle repair begins with re-approximation of the posterior crura with interrupted ethabon sutures.
In this case, four sutures have been placed to re-approximate this very large posterior hiatus.
Anterior sutures here were also necessary to help reduce the defect.
A biological mesh, which has been measured and cut on the back table, is then placed into the abdomen and tacked to the diaphragm with interrupted ethobond sutures.
The sutured mesh covers approximately 270 degrees of the hiatal closure without constricting it too tightly.
An anterior fundoplication is performed by tacking the fundus of the stomach to the esophagus as shown here.
Tension is removed by the assistant prior to tying down the knots.
The left side of the fundus is similarly approximated to the left side of the esophagus, again taking care to reduce tension while tying the knots.
Here you can clearly see the intra-abdominal neoesophagus created by the gastroplasty performed earlier.
The anterior stitches are taken from the fundus directly to the diaphragm without incorporating any of the esophagus.

two sutures are placed in this case with the repair complete the liver retractor may be removed under direct visualization and all port sites closed in standard fashion post-operatively the patient had a swallow exam which revealed normal contrast transit time through the stomach
